title: Release Management
scan: What you will learn: How to select the right branching strategy (trunk-based, GitHub Flow, GitFlow), enforce commit hygiene, govern PRs at scale, lock down branches with protection rules, choose a promotion pattern, and version releases correctly

Why it matters: Branching strategy is the single most impactful development-process decision on a multi-team project — a wrong choice creates merge conflicts, deployment coupling, and release bottlenecks that cannot be fixed without a full process migration
Trunk-based development is the correct default for AEMaaCS projects — long-lived feature branches are incompatible with Cloud Manager's continuous deployment model
A PR with no description, no linked ticket, and more than 400 lines changed is not reviewable — it is a delivery risk that looks like progress
Hypercare windows must be defined before go-live, not after the first incident

blocks.html: elease management starts in Git. Before there's a deploy pipeline, a rollback, or a hypercare plan, there's a branching strategy and a PR discipline. Get those right and most of release management gets easy; get them wrong and no amount of CI/CD tooling will save you.

The R of CODER is the discipline that decides when things go live — and equally, what gets rolled back when they shouldn't have. Most digital outages aren't engineering failures; they're release failures. This deep-dive starts with Git (where every release actually originates), then covers the branching strategy, the PR mechanics, promotion gates, and versioning.

sections.title: Branching strategy · pick one, stick to it

blocks.html: There are essentially three patterns the industry uses. The right one depends on team size, deploy cadence, and how regulated the environment is.

cards.title: One long-lived branch
cards.body: Everyone commits to main, behind feature flags for incomplete work. Short-lived branches (hours, not weeks) for individual PRs. Fits: high-velocity teams, mature CI, feature-flag infra. Default for SaaS.

cards.title: Main + feature branches
cards.body: main is always deployable. Branch off main for each feature, PR, merge back. No long-lived release branches. Fits: most agency work, mid-sized teams, weekly-to-monthly releases.

cards.title: Multiple long-lived branches
cards.body: main, develop, release/*, feature/*, hotfix/*. Complex but explicit. Fits: regulated environments, infrequent releases, multiple parallel versions in production. Wrong for most agency work — it's heavy ceremony for problems most teams don't have.

blocks.html: How to decide: deploy daily or more → trunk-based. Deploy weekly or biweekly → GitHub Flow. Deploy quarterly with audit gates → GitFlow. If you can't answer the deploy-cadence question, that's the first conversation to have.

blocks.source:
gitGraph
   commit id: "Sprint 17 baseline"
   branch feature/cart-redesign
   checkout feature/cart-redesign
   commit id: "add cart state machine"
   commit id: "add cart render"
   checkout main
   merge feature/cart-redesign
   commit id: "tag: v2.14.0"

items: Branch naming — type/ticket-id-short-description. Examples: feat/PROJ-1234-add-search, fix/PROJ-5678-cart-total, chore/upgrade-react-19. Predictable, sortable, traceable.
Conventional Commits — feat:, fix:, chore:, docs:, refactor:, test:, perf:, build:. Powers automated changelog and version bumping. Worth the small discipline.
Atomic commits — one logical change per commit. A revertable unit. Don't bundle "refactor + new feature + lint fix" into one commit; future-you will hate present-you when something goes wrong.
Commit body for non-trivial changes — why, not what. The diff shows what; the body shows the reasoning.

blocks.label: a commit message that earns its keep
blocks.code:
feat(checkout): coalesce payment retries into single attempt

Previously, payment failures triggered three independent retries from
the UI, occasionally hitting the gateway four times for one customer
intent. Now retries are coalesced server-side with a 5-minute idempotency
key, preventing duplicate captures.

Refs: INC-2891
Co-authored-by: ...

sections.title: Pull requests · the unit of integration

blocks.html: A PR is the moment your code becomes everyone's code. The mechanics determine whether reviews are useful or theatre.

items: Size · under 400 lines changed is the empirical sweet spot. Above that, review quality collapses; reviewers skim instead of think. Big features → multiple small PRs behind a feature flag.
Draft early — open a draft PR as soon as the shape is there. Get directional feedback before investing in polish.
PR description as documentation — what changed, why, how to test, what was deliberately not done. The first commit message for the squash-merge.
Self-review before requesting review — open your own PR diff. You'll catch half the issues yourself.
One approval + green CI — the standard gate. Two approvals for sensitive areas (auth, payments, data migrations).
Review SLA — agree on it. "PRs reviewed within 1 business day" or the team gets stuck. Aging PRs are tech debt accumulating.

blocks.html: DEPT® field pattern: enforce a PR size limit of 400 lines via a CI check. PRs that exceed this limit are auto-labelled "needs-split" and blocked from merge until split or explicitly overridden by the architect with a documented justification. A 400-line PR takes 45 minutes to review well; an 800-line PR takes 2 hours and produces superficial review.
Configuration on the platform (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket) that enforces the policy the team agreed to.

items: Require PR before merge — no direct pushes to main. Ever.
Require status checks — CI green, security scans clean, coverage above floor.
Require approvals — at least one, typically. CODEOWNERS for sensitive paths automatically requests the right reviewers.
Dismiss stale reviews — when new commits land after approval, reset the approval. Avoids silent slipping of unrelated changes past review.
No force-push to main — ever, for any reason. History is the audit trail.
Signed commits — required in regulated environments. GPG or platform signing.
Linear history — squash-merge or rebase-merge only. Merge commits make git bisect harder than it needs to be.

blocks.html:
Before

No branch protection on main. A developer force-pushes over a release tag during an incident. The tag is gone; the previous release cannot be reproduced.

After

Branch protection requires 2 approvals and passing status checks; history rewrites are blocked; tags are protected. The incident never happens.

sections.title: Promotion · how a change moves from feature branch to production

blocks.html: Promotion is what turns a Git event into a customer-visible release. The pattern depends on the branching strategy.

blocks.title: Trunk-based promotion · every merge is a release candidate

items: Build once, promote many — the same artefact moves through environments. Don't rebuild per environment; configuration differs, code does not.
Promotion gates are explicit — automated tests at the gate to stage; manual approval at the gate to prod (unless you're confident enough in automation to skip it).
Rollback is a first-class operation — deploying the previous artefact must be a button press, not a hotfix branch. If rolling back is harder than rolling forward, the system isn't actually safe.

cards.title: Two production environments, one live
cards.body: Deploy to the dark stack; smoke test; flip the router. Rollback = flip back. Doubles infra cost; halves rollback time.

cards.title: Roll out to a small slice first
cards.body: 1% → 10% → 50% → 100%, with metrics watched at each step. Catches issues before full exposure. Requires good observability.

cards.title: Code deployed, feature off
cards.body: Decouple deploy from release. Flip the flag on for 1% of users; measure; expand. The pattern that makes trunk-based feasible.

sections.title: Merge vs rebase · the argument resolved

columns.label: Rebase your own branch
columns.body: While the PR is open and only you've touched it. Keeps history linear, removes interleaved noise. The right move before you ask for review.

columns.label: Merge to integrate
columns.body: Squash-merge (collapse to one commit) or merge-commit (preserve the branch history) when bringing the PR into main. Don't rebase a shared branch — anyone else who pulled it gets a corrupted history.

blocks.html: Squash vs merge-commit at PR time: squash gives one tidy commit per PR (clean main history); merge-commit preserves intra-PR commits (richer history, more noise). Most teams pick squash and treat the PR description as the canonical message.

Using git merge instead of git rebase for updating feature branches in a trunk-based workflow. Merge commits create a non-linear history. git bisect becomes unreliable; git blame becomes misleading; the merge commit hides the actual change behind a "Merge branch X into main" entry. Rebase to a linear history; squash to a logical commit.

sections.title: Versioning · what version are we on?

blocks.html: Two conventions matter. Pick the right one for the artefact.

columns.label: SemVer · MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH
columns.body: Libraries, APIs, SDKs. MAJOR breaks compatibility. MINOR adds capability. PATCH fixes bugs. Consumers can pin and predict.

columns.label: CalVer · YYYY.MM.PATCH
columns.body: Applications, SaaS, browser releases. The version tells you when it shipped. Useful when the question "is this from before or after X?" matters more than "is this compatible?"

items: Release tags — every deploy gets an immutable Git tag (v1.4.2 or 2026.03.05). Tag is the audit anchor.
Deployment tags — separate from release tags. "v1.4.2 → prod 2026-03-05T14:30" is a deployment event, not a code event.
Automated bumping — Conventional Commits + semantic-release (or equivalent) reads the commit history and bumps the version automatically. Removes the human "what version is this?" conversation.
Changelog generated from commits. Belongs in the release artefact, not in someone's email.

blocks.html: Release isn't done when the deploy completes. It's done after hypercare expires.

items: Hypercare window — defined per release. Typically 24–72 hours of heightened monitoring after a non-trivial change.
Named on-call — for the hypercare window, a named engineer is on the hook. Not "the team."
Rollback decision authority — agreed in advance. Who can push the button without a meeting? When is a meeting required?
Post-release verification — automated checks beyond "site is up." Business KPIs (conversions, signups) should be the strongest signal that the release is good.

blocks.html: A team that cannot describe its branching strategy on a whiteboard in 30 seconds cannot manage releases at scale. The Git diagram is the release management diagram.

blocks.label: Architect's Review · Release Management
items: What branching strategy is in use, and does it match the deploy cadence?
Are PRs typically under 400 lines, or are they larger and rubber-stamped?
What is the PR review SLA, and is it being met?
Are branch protection rules in place: required reviews, status checks, no force-push, signed commits where required?
Is the same artefact promoted across environments, or rebuilt each time?
What is the rollback procedure, and when was it last tested?
Which deploy pattern is in use — blue-green, canary, feature-flag — and does it match the risk profile?
Is versioning automated from commit conventions, or assigned by hand?
Is there a defined hypercare window, with named on-call and a rollback authority?
Are business KPIs (not just availability metrics) used to verify a successful release?
